perturbation.original_text: Accepted for and on behalf of (a) (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) Signed Dated Signed Dated Signed Dated
perturbation.changed_text: I/We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address) Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention) and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name) Accepted for and on behalf of (a) (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) Signed Dated Signed Dated Signed Dated
perturbation.explanation: Placing the signature block at the end makes it appear as if the confidentiality agreement is merely a suggestion, rather than a legally binding obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: U.C.C. § 2-204 requires 'conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract.' By placing the signature block, which is meant to provide evidence of the existence of the contract, after the obligation, makes the conduct appear less certain, thereby failing to establish a contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the placement of the signature block in a way that undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. The Perturbation Explanation states that placing the signature block at the end makes the agreement appear as merely a suggestion. The Law Explanation states that U.C.C. § 2-204 requires conduct that recognizes the existence of a contract, and placing the signature block after the obligation makes the conduct appear less certain. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that a contract may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. By placing the signature block after the agreement, it weakens the evidence that both parties intended to enter into a binding contract, creating a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: Signed Dated Signed Dated Signed Dated This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.explanation: Moving the exceptions to the confidentiality obligation to after the signature block suggests the signature confirms the non-disclosure agreement, but not necessarily the conditions surrounding the agreement, thereby making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 211 addresses standardized agreements. Moving exceptions after the signature reduces the probability of the other party 'understand[ing] that they agree to the terms of the writing', thus making the exceptions difficult to enforce. This would only be valid if the other party has a reasonable expectation of such terms, but this placement makes this unlikely.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Restatement (Second) of Contracts - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Widely cited American legal treatise The Restatement (Second) of the Law of Contracts is a legal treatise from the second series of the Restatements of the Law , and seeks to inform judges and lawyers about general principles of contract common law. It is one of the best-recognized and frequently cited legal treatises [ 1 ] in all of American jurisprudence . Every first-year law student in the United States is exposed to it, and it is a frequently cited non-binding authority in all of U.S. common law in the areas of contracts and commercial transactions. [ 2 ] The American Law Institute began work on the second edition in 1962 and completed it in 1979; the version in use at present has a copyright year of 1981. [ 3 ] For an explanation of the purpose of a restatement of law, see Restatement of the Law . Use and commentary [ edit ] Legal scholars and jurists have commented extensively on the Restatement, both in contrasting it with aspects of the first Restatement, and in evaluating its influence and effectiveness in reaching its stated objectives. It is in this context of direct review that one can find numerous arguments both favoring and criticizing some aspects of the Restatement as an independent source of legal scholarship. [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] Although several sections of the Restatement contained new rules which sometimes contradicted existing law, courts citing these sections have predominantly adopted the Restatement's view, citing them as a court would cite statute or code. [ 9 ] Far more common, however, is the practice of citing the Restatement to clarify generally accepted doctrine in every major area of contract and commercial law. It is in this context of legal research that one can find the Restatement used as direct substantiation and persuasive authority , to validate the arguments and interpretations of individual legal practitioners. [ 10 ] [ 11 ] Legacy [ edit ] Although the Restatement of Contracts is still an influential academic work, certain aspects have been superseded in everyday legal practice by the Uniform Commercial Code . Specifically, the UCC has replaced the Restatement (Second) of Contracts in regard to the sale of goods. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts remains the unofficial authority for aspects of contract law which find their genesis in the common law principles of the United States and, previously, England. perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that placing the exceptions after the signature block could make enforcement difficult because it suggests the signature doesn't apply to the conditions. The Law Explanation uses Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 211 to argue that this placement reduces the probability of the other party understanding and agreeing to the terms, making the exceptions hard to enforce. The altered text moves the exceptions after the signature block, which weakens the agreement's enforceability by potentially obscuring the conditions of confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Restatement (Second) of Contracts as a frequently cited authority in contract law, supporting the Law Explanation's basis for the legal standard. Thus, the structural flaw undermines the enforceability of the agreement, creating a legal contradiction.
